serving.description: 2 slices of wholemeal bread with peanut butter and jam
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 100 g, equivalent to two slices of wholemeal bread spread with peanut butter and jam.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal bread with peanut butter and jam — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for wholemeal bread with peanut butter and jam (cr_id F05020144). All per-100g values preserved from the HPB record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wholemeal / Whole-wheat Bread — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 (medium) is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for wholemeal bread (GI range 60–74). The composite dish GI is dominated by the wholemeal bread component; peanut butter lowers the effective GI slightly while jam raises it. The net GI of 69 is a defensible estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database wholemeal bread reference. Glycemic load of 27.6 calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 39.96) / 100 ≈ 27.6 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 306 mg/100 g is borderline elevated; partially offset by dietary fibre 4.82 g/100 g and potassium 93 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and choose low-sodium peanut butter and reduced-sugar jam to keep cumulative sodium intake within the 2,000 mg/day target; the wholegrain fibre content provides modest cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 69 and carbohydrate 39.96 g/100 g yield a glycemic load of ~27.6; dietary fibre 4.82 g and protein 12.17 g moderate postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to one slice (approximately 50 g), choose no-added-sugar jam, and pair with a protein source to further blunt the glycemic response; monitoring postprandial blood glucose is advised given the medium GI and moderate glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary fibre 4.82 g/100 g and unsaturated fats from peanut butter support cardiovascular health; saturated fat estimated at ~1.8 g/100 g is well below the 5 g threshold; cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This food is broadly suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its high fibre content, low saturated fat, and zero cholesterol; however, sodium at 306 mg/100 g warrants attention and low-sodium peanut butter variants are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal bread with peanut butter and jam is a popular everyday food consumed widely in Singapore and internationally as a breakfast or snack item. It consists of sliced wholemeal (whole-wheat) bread spread with peanut butter and fruit jam. The wholemeal bread provides a dense, slightly nutty texture and a mild wheaty flavour, while peanut butter contributes a rich, creamy, savoury-sweet taste and jam adds sweetness and fruitiness. Nutritionally, per 100 g this food delivers 295 kcal, 12.17 g protein, 9.94 g fat, 39.96 g carbohydrate, 4.82 g dietary fibre, and 306 mg sodium. The high fibre content from wholemeal bread supports digestive health and moderates glycemic response. Peanut butter contributes unsaturated fats, vitamin E, and plant protein. The glycemic index is medium at 69, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 27.6 per 100 g. Clinically, the combination is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor carbohydrate load and choose no-added-sugar jam; those with hypertension should select low-sodium peanut butter. The food is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and contains gluten and peanut allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020144) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 69 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published ranges for wholemeal bread. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05020144. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported fields is not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: peanut butter from non-hydrogenated sources and wholemeal bread do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per HPB and USDA FoodData Central references. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central component data for wholemeal bread and natural peanut butter in typical proportions; this is an approximation as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 per HPB source — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based composite with no animal-derived ingredients. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database wholemeal bread reference values (GI range 60–74); the composite GI reflects the dominant wholemeal bread component modulated by peanut butter (lowers GI) and jam (raises GI).
